title: Cloud Drain
shortDescription: Crypto Mining via Stolen Cloud Credentials

briefing.incidentSummary:
A startup received an AWS bill of ₹14.2 lakh for a single month — 60x their usual spend.

Someone had spun up 47 high-performance GPU instances in three regions and was running cryptocurrency mining software around the clock.

The AWS root credentials were leaked. But by who — and how?
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Trace how the credentials were leaked

› Identify who accessed and misused them

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Ketan S
suspects.profileNotes: Pushed AWS root credentials to a public GitHub repo 3 months ago inside a .env file. Instead of immediately rotating the credentials, he quietly removed the commit. A bot scraped the credentials within 8 minutes of the push. Ketan then used the still-valid credentials himself to mine crypto from home.

suspects.name: Automated Credential Bot
suspects.profileNotes: Scraped the credentials within 8 minutes of the GitHub push. However the mining activity began 11 days later — suggesting the human actor (Ketan S) used the credentials himself rather than the bot operator.

suspects.name: Prerna A
suspects.profileNotes: Had legitimate AWS access but only to staging environments. No GPU instance provisioning rights. No activity outside business hours.

suspects.name: Aman V
suspects.profileNotes: Internship ended 5 months ago. AWS access revoked on last day. No activity under his credentials since then.

items.label: oops pushed .env with keys, removing now
items.detail: Git commit message from 3 months ago. Ketan pushed AWS_ACCESS_KEY and AWS_SECRET_KEY in a .env file then immediately deleted the commit — but GitHub history retains the data, and a scraper had already captured it within 8 minutes.

items.label: Why is our AWS bill ₹14 lakh?? Who authorized GPU instances??
items.detail: Prerna's message when the bill alert fired. She immediately raised the alarm and began the investigation. Not involved in the breach.

items.label:
user_data: #!/bin/bash
apt install -y xmrig
xmrig --config /tmp/pool.conf
items.detail: User data script embedded in all 47 GPU instance launches. Installs XMRig — a Monero mining tool — immediately on boot. The pool config points to a mining pool wallet traced to Ketan S.

items.label: aws_cloudtrail_log.json
items.detail: AWS CloudTrail log shows root account API calls from IP 103.55.77.12 starting 11 days after the credential leak. 47 p3.8xlarge GPU instances launched across us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-south-1 over 4 hours. All launched using root credentials — not IAM roles.

items.label: github_secret_scan_report.txt
items.detail: GitHub's secret scanning flagged the .env commit 9 minutes after push — but Ketan had already deleted it 1 minute prior. The 8-minute scraper window means the credentials were captured before deletion. Ketan never rotated them.

items.label: mining_wallet_analysis.pdf
items.detail: XMRig pool wallet traced via blockchain analysis to a Monero address that had previously received payouts to an exchange account KYC-verified under Ketan S's PAN card. ₹1.8 lakh in mining revenue identified.

hiddenItem.label: vpn_exit_node_trace.txt
hiddenItem.detail: VPN exit node IP 103.55.77.12 linked to a commercial VPN service. Subscriber account registered under ketan.sharma.dev@gmail.com — Ketan S's known developer email. Confirms the AWS API calls came from his machine.

minigame.title: Decode VPN Subscriber Username
minigame.hint: Caesar shift -3. Whose VPN account is this?
hints: It is a developer's name followed by their initial.

onSuccess.message: vpn_exit_node_trace.txt is now available in your file feed.

items.label: Leak to Launch Timeline
items.detail: Credentials leaked via GitHub at 11:14 PM. Bot scraped them at 11:22 PM. Mining activity began 11 days later at 2:00 AM — suggesting a human who knew the credentials were valid used them deliberately, not the bot.

items.label: GPU Instance Spread
items.detail: 47 p3.8xlarge instances across 3 AWS regions — designed to stay under each region's default vCPU limit to avoid triggering automatic AWS abuse detection. Shows awareness of AWS internal thresholds — someone with cloud knowledge.

items.label: AWS API Call Origin
items.detail: All 47 RunInstances API calls originated from IP 103.55.77.12 — a commercial VPN exit node. The same IP was used for all subsequent instance management calls. VPN subscriber records link it to Ketan S.

timeline.title: Credentials Pushed to GitHub
timeline.description: Ketan S commits .env file with AWS root keys

timeline.title: Bot Scrapes Credentials
timeline.description: Automated scraper captures keys 8 minutes after push

timeline.title: Commit Deleted
timeline.description: Ketan removes the commit — but never rotates the keys

timeline.title: Mining Campaign Launched
timeline.description: 47 GPU instances spun up across 3 AWS regions

timeline.title: Bill Alert Fires
timeline.description: AWS sends ₹14.2 lakh billing alert to startup

timeline.title: CloudTrail Pulled
timeline.description: API logs reveal root credential abuse from VPN IP

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Ketan S exposed as the cloud mining fraudster.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Ketan S.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...₹14 lakh. In one month. For 47 machines nobody authorised.
The keys were out there. Someone knew — and used them.
Find who knew.
aria.exploreFeeds:
CloudTrail logs. GitHub history. Blockchain traces.
Cloud crimes leave receipts. Read them.
aria.viewEvidence:
The keys were never rotated. 11 days passed before launch.
This wasn't a random bot. This was deliberate.
aria.decryptionHint:
The VPN account has a name behind it.
Decode the subscriber username.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
